title_en: Health Influencers, Trust, and Correction Fatigue on Short-Video Platforms
title_zh: Health Influencers, Trust, and Correction Fatigue on Short-Video Platforms
abstract_en: The study combines experiments and surveys to test when health influencers can successfully correct misleading claims on short-video platforms. It finds that source trust matters, but correction messages lose force when users feel repeatedly asked to evaluate similar claims in rapid succession.
abstract_zh: The study combines experiments and surveys to test when health influencers can successfully correct misleading claims on short-video platforms. It finds that source trust matters, but correction messages lose force when users feel repeatedly asked to evaluate similar claims in rapid succession.
plain_summary_zh: This paper studies a practical problem: if health influencers are constantly correcting misinformation, do audiences eventually tune out? The findings suggest that trust helps corrections work, but repeated correction can also create fatigue and reduce willingness to engage.
plain_summary_en: This paper studies a practical problem: if health influencers are constantly correcting misinformation, do audiences eventually tune out? The findings suggest that trust helps corrections work, but repeated correction can also create fatigue and reduce willingness to engage.
research_direction_zh: It links health communication, platform trust, and misinformation correction, showing that effective correction depends not only on accuracy but also on audience fatigue.
research_direction_en: It links health communication, platform trust, and misinformation correction, showing that effective correction depends not only on accuracy but also on audience fatigue.
raw_title: Health Influencers, Trust, and Correction Fatigue on Short-Video Platforms
raw_abstract: The study combines experiments and surveys to test when health influencers can successfully correct misleading claims on short-video platforms. It finds that source trust matters, but correction messages lose force when users feel repeatedly asked to evaluate similar claims in rapid succession.
